summary: Gruber responds to Bill Bumgarner's excitement about Mac OS X 10.2.4's installer being able to find applications by bundle identifier rather than hard-coded paths. Gruber points out that classic Mac OS installers had this capability over a decade earlier, making the celebration misplaced. He argues this isn't a new feature worth praising but rather the belated elimination of a deficiency that should never have existed in OS X. He also notes that the problem of resolving multiple apps with the same identifier was already solved in the classic Mac era with a simple dialog.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.2.4's installer gained the ability to find applications by bundle identifier rather than fixed paths
Classic Mac OS had equivalent functionality since the early 1990s, making this a regression that was finally corrected rather than an innovation
The OS X installer should have worked this way from version 10.0 onward
The problem of multiple matching applications was already solved in classic Mac OS with a simple user dialog
money_quotes: I thought this was pretty cool, too. The first time I saw it, over 10 years ago.
Mac users have been accustomed to installers and updaters that can find older versions of applications anywhere, on any local volume, since the early 90's.

That this ability to locate applications using a method other than hard-coded paths is new to the built-in Mac OS X installer isn't cause for celebration; it's just one less idiotic deficiency to complain about.
There's no reason whatsoever it shouldn't have been like this ever since 10.0.

If it's anything like what Mac users have been used to for the past 10 years, the solution is simple: display a dialog listing each matching application, and ask the user which copy they want to update.
key_insight: Features that existed in classic Mac OS and were lost in the transition to OS X shouldn't be celebrated when they're finally restored — they should be expected.
